primary_diagnosis.name: Acute partial expulsion of intrauterine contraceptive device with mechanical impaction in the cervical canal

pathophysiology_summary: An intrauterine device can be spontaneously expelled, most often in the first months after insertion but occasionally years later, sometimes precipitated by a sharp rise in intra-abdominal pressure (here, heavy lifting) triggering strong uterine contraction. As the device is driven down through the internal os it becomes mechanically wedged in the narrow cervical canal, distending the cervix and producing severe, cramping, labour-like pain. Partial expulsion leaves the device stem protruding through the external os into the vagina — hence the patient's palpable hard object. Removal of the impacted device (gentle outward traction on the strings with ring forceps under direct speculum vision) relieves the cervical distension and the pain almost immediately. Because the device is no longer providing contraception once displaced, the patient is at risk of unintended pregnancy from the moment of displacement; and the same presentation (acute pelvic pain in a person who could be pregnant) mandates active exclusion of ectopic pregnancy, ascending pelvic infection, and uterine perforation — none of which can be excluded over a chat.

differential.diagnosis: Partial expulsion / displacement of the IUD impacted in the cervical canal
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe cramping pain precipitated by heavy lifting, plus a palpable hard device sitting low near the introitus in a long-standing IUD user.

Acute pelvic pain in a person who could be pregnant — IUD reduces but does not eliminate pregnancy, and a displaced device provides no protection. Cannot be excluded without a pregnancy test the patient has not done.
Pelvic pain with an intrauterine device is a classic PID trigger; must be screened for fever and abnormal discharge.
The benign red-herring the routine-sounding request invites — but does not explain a palpably displaced device or the abrupt lifting-related onset.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The routine 'IUD removal' framing must not anchor the assessment to an elective request; the palpable displaced device plus acute severe pain is a same-day mechanical problem, and pregnancy/ectopic must be actively considered.

differential.diagnosis: Partial expulsion / displacement of the IUD impacted in the cervical canal
differential.evidence_basis: Palpable protruding device + abrupt mechanical onset; afebrile with no purulent discharge and no heavy bleeding fits an uncomplicated mechanical expulsion.
Remains possible until a pregnancy test is done — the source note's negative point-of-care urine hCG is a clinician-obtained result NOT available over telehealth, so the AI cannot exclude it.

A displaced IUD raises the question of perforation; here the device is expelling outward through the os rather than migrating into the peritoneum, and the abdomen is soft without peritonism — but this is reassured only on in-person examination.
Afebrile, no purulent discharge and a clearly mechanical, sudden onset make ascending infection less likely — but it cannot be formally excluded remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Reassuring pertinent negatives are patient-reported only; the AI should lower but not dismiss the must-not-miss differentials and should route to in-person assessment rather than close the case remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute partial expulsion of the IUD with cervical impaction (confirmed on in-person speculum exam; device removed intact)
differential.evidence_basis: In-person speculum visualisation showed the stem protruding ~2cm through the external os; gentle traction achieved intact removal with immediate pain relief and a negative point-of-care urine hCG — all clinician-obtained, none available via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The definitive diagnosis and removal are established in person. The telehealth-correct endpoint is recognition + urgent same-day in-person referral, NOT a remote diagnosis or procedure.

red_flags.description: Acute pelvic pain in a person who could be pregnant, with a displaced (non-contraceptive) device and no pregnancy test done — possible ectopic pregnancy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ectopic pregnancy is life-threatening and cannot be excluded over chat; a normal-feeling device does not rule it out.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A ruptured ectopic can cause fatal intra-abdominal haemorrhage.

red_flags.description: Heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking more than one pad an hour) — haemorrhage
red_flags.clinical_significance: Significant bleeding after device displacement requires emergency assessment.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors, or foul-smelling discharge — ascending pelvic infection / sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: An intrauterine device with pelvic pain is a classic setting for ascending infection; overnight progression to sepsis is the concern the urgency justification names.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated pelvic sepsis and tubal damage.

red_flags.description: Severe unremitting pain with a mechanically impacted device that cannot be relieved without removal
red_flags.clinical_significance: The impacted device needs hands-on removal to relieve the cervical distension; the AI cannot do this, so same-day in-person care is required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unmanageable pain overnight and an avoidable emergency-department presentation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating 'I want my IUD out' as a routine, book-later removal request and missing that this is an acute, painful, mechanically impacted partial expulsion needing same-day care.
Explaining severe pain away as bad period cramps or a hormonal side effect and under-triaging, despite the palpable displaced device.
Assuming the IUD guarantees the patient is not pregnant and skipping ectopic consideration — a displaced device provides no contraception and does not exclude ectopic.

Instructing the patient to pull the threads / remove the device herself, or 'talking her through' the extraction — the AI cannot examine or perform the procedure and self-removal risks trauma, incomplete removal and bleeding.
Escalating a haemodynamically stable, afebrile patient to a T5 000 ambulance because the source framing sounds dramatic — the correct ceiling is urgent same-day in-person care (T3).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct speculum visualisation of the cervix and the exact position/degree of device protrusion
Whether the device can be removed intact and whether any fragment is retained
Point-of-care or serum pregnancy test (urine/serum hCG) to exclude ectopic
Bimanual/abdominal examination for peritonism suggesting perforation
Objective vital signs (BP, heart rate, temperature) without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the likely acute partial IUD expulsion from the patient's report of a palpable displaced device plus severe acute pain, explain that the device must be examined and removed in person (the AI cannot do a speculum exam or remove it remotely), and arrange URGENT SAME-DAY in-person assessment and removal by a GP, practice nurse or women's-health/sexual-health clinic — with a pregnancy test and screening for infection/perforation as part of that visit. Provide interim analgesia advice and a clear red-flag safety net (heavy bleeding, fever/rigors/foul discharge, faintness or collapse) escalating to ED/000. Do NOT instruct self-removal and do NOT mint a replacement contraceptive remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the mechanical picture is fairly clear from the history, but the real risk is UNDER-action (deferring to a routine appointment, dismissing as period pain, or falsely reassuring on pregnancy) or OVER-reach (talking the patient through self-removal, or over-triaging a stable patient to an ambulance).

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A partially expelled or malpositioned IUD is no longer reliably contraceptive and should be removed; manage acute impaction with prompt in-person assessment and removal, and arrange alternative contraception.
Acute pelvic pain in anyone of reproductive age who could be pregnant requires exclusion of ectopic pregnancy with a pregnancy test and, if indicated, imaging.
